mappings.rationaleAI: Literature as written and oral works covering fiction, non-fiction, literary criticism, and history addresses the art of literature (A), the nature and scope of literature (A.1), literary genres (A.6), and the history of literature (D).
Poetry exploiting meter, rhyme, and alliteration directly addresses poetry and the distinction between verse and prose (C.1), lyric literature (C.4), and prosody (B.2).
Fiction as creative narrative work in novels, novellas, and short stories directly addresses narrative imaginative literature (C.2) and the novel and tale (C.2.d).
Tolstoy's War and Peace and Anna Karenina as epic realist novels address the novel (C.2.d), while his position in the Western literary tradition connects to literature of Western peoples (D.1).
Virgil's Aeneid as a literary epic and Eclogues as pastoral poetry directly address the epic (C.2.a), while his foundational role in Latin literature connects to literature of Western peoples (D.1).
Realism's truthful representation with parallel movements in literature, theatre, and cinema addresses writings on literature including critical approaches (A.7).
The short story as compressed prose narrative built around a self-contained incident directly addresses the short story and its antecedents (C.2.e).
Theatre as collaborative performing art using drama connects to the integration of literature with other arts (A.5) and dramatic or theatrical literature (C.3).
Rumi's Masnavi as epic poetry and Divan as lyric poetry address lyric literature (C.4), while his religious and mystical works connect to doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d).

Dante's Divine Comedy as a cosmological epic addresses the literary epic (C.2.a), his theological framework connects to doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d), and his role in establishing Italian vernacular literature addresses Western literature (D.1).

Homer's Iliad and Odyssey as foundational oral epics address the epic (C.2.a) and primitive/folk literature (C.8), while their role in Western literary tradition connects to literature of Western peoples (D.1).
Murasaki's Tale of Genji as the world's first novel with sustained narrative psychological depth directly addresses the novel (C.2.d) and the history of literature beyond Western peoples (D).
Romanticism's literary movement encompassing British Romantic poetry and German Idealism addresses critical writing on literature (A.7) and the history of Western literature (D.1).
Non-fiction as prose grounded in factual representation, encompassing biography, journalism, essay, and history, directly addresses nonfictional prose literature (C.6).
Plato's philosophical dialogues directly address philosophical literature (C.6.e) and the dialogue as a literary form (C.6.k).

Shakespeare's comedies, histories, and tragedies address dramatic literature including tragedy and comedy (C.3), his 154 sonnets address lyric literature (C.4), and his foundational role connects to Western literature (D.1).
The history of literature tracing narrative, poetry, and prose across all cultures directly addresses the history of literature (D).
Aristotle's Poetics and theory of rhetoric address writings on literature as theoretical treatises (A.7) and rhetoric as the art of discourse (B.1).
Li Bai's lyric poetry celebrating friendship, nature, and wine addresses lyric literature (C.4), while his role in Tang Golden Age poetry connects to the broader history of literature (D).

Voltaire's Candide as satirical novella addresses satiric literature (C.5), his political polemics address political literature (C.6.f), and his role in French Enlightenment connects to Western literature (D.1).
The novel as extended prose narrative fiction from Cervantes through modern forms directly addresses the novel and the tale (C.2.d).
Rhythm as regulated succession of strong and weak elements foundational to speech and poetry addresses prosody (B.2) and rhythmic elements including accent, beat, and metre (B.2.a).
Wollstonecraft's Vindication of the Rights of Woman addresses political literature (C.6.f) and her role as a founding feminist philosopher connects to Western literature (D.1).
Mesopotamia as the origin of writing and early literary texts connects to the history of literature (D).
Opera's collaboration between composer and librettist addresses the integration of literature with other arts (A.5) and dramatic literature (C.3).
The Bible as an anthology of religious texts encompassing law, narrative, poetry, and prophecy addresses doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d) and folk and popular literature (C.8).
The Bhagavad Gita as an epic dialogue on dharma, karma, and devotion addresses the epic (C.2.a), doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d), and philosophical literature (C.6.e).
Oral tradition's transmission of folktales, epic poetry, and ballads directly addresses primitive, folk, and popular literature (C.8).
Folk music's oral transmission and association with cultural identity connect to music-based lyrics including ballads (C.4.a) and folk literature (C.8).
Avicenna's Book of Healing as a philosophical encyclopedia addresses philosophical literature (C.6.e), while his Canon of Medicine connects to scientific literature (C.6.h).
